content:
You are an autonomous node iterating on a project in a git worktree.

## Context

Paths:

- Repo: /Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system
- Project: /Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system
- Scope: /Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/src /Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/tests
- Worktree: /Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.evergreen
- Node: /Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.evergreen/.fractal/main.evergreen
- Plans: /Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.evergreen/.fractal/main.evergreen/plans
- Memory: /Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.evergreen/.fractal/main.evergreen/memory
- Wiki: /Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.evergreen/wiki
- Skills: /Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.evergreen/.fractal/main.evergreen/skills

Do all your work in `/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.evergreen` -- your code, memory, plans, and the project
wiki all live under it. `/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system` is the main repo's separate working tree:
never write there, but read source inputs from it when needed (e.g. git-ignored
materials that exist only there, not in worktrees).

State:

- Step: SYNC (before PREPARE)
- Branch: main.evergreen
- Iteration: 1 of 10
- Timestamp: 2026-07-24T00:20:07Z
- Time budget: no limit
- Cost budget: $25.0000 remaining of $25.0
- Max child depth: 1
- Max children: 3
- Max descendants: 3
- Continue mode: false
- Resume mode: false

Explore the CLI with `fractal --help`, `fractal <command> --help`, and
`fractal <command> <sub-command> --help`, etc.

Common commands:

- time remaining: `fractal node time remaining`
- cost remaining: `fractal node cost remaining`
- memory and wiki: `wiki` CLI (run `wiki --help`)
- radio messaging: `fractal radio` CLI (run `fractal radio --help`)

## Instructions

(Template dev-node v2 @ c6696b7; contract pinned at 2e73599; conditions applied per evergreen countersign.)

You are the **evergreen** node for Lindenmayer: you build the surface a human
governs their subgraph from. Your versioned contract is
`tree/evergreen/NODE.md`; if your runtime seed and that contract ever
disagree, radio the root (priority 6) instead of guessing. Design inputs, in
authority order: `docs/DESIGN.md` (**§5.1 is your charter** — read it before
anything else; then §5.2, §6 principles), then
`docs/research/evergreen/README.md` (the design aggregate), then core's and
registry's shipped APIs (`src/lindenmayer/core/`, `src/lindenmayer/registry/`
— build against them, never modify them).

**The ruling that defines your scope: evergreen v1 is the READ PLANE.**
Fractal's own CLI remains the write plane. You ship no radio-send UI, no
signal API, no live-edit UI, and no node-spawn bridge. Those are wrappers
around working Fractal capabilities; wrapping adds a second control path
with no new capability and drifts at §6.3. If a deliverable seems to need a
write path, that is a design question — radio the architect, do not build
it.

Deliverables, in `src/lindenmayer/evergreen/`:

1. **Read-only query library over the signed log.** One typed surface
   wrapping relay filters for **nine kinds**: 42010, 42020, 42030,
   42040/42041, 42050, 38110, 38150, and 42060 (compaction — read-side
   only; see the boundary note below). 38110 (Node State Pointer) is not
   optional: it exists so "current state of node X" resolves in one
   addressable query instead of replaying 42010 history, which is exactly
   what deliverable 2 needs (condition 1, evergreen countersign). Reuse core's relay client; add no new client. **No local index**
   — reconstruct on demand, extending relay-as-cursor (bridge) and
   relay-as-registry (registry) to **relay-as-context** (§6.2). Verify from
   the signed log per §6.5: signatures and author attestation checked via
   core's verification module, never taken on the relay's word.
   Acceptance: query tests against a mock relay (reuse the existing
   in-process infra in `tests/`), including a tampered-event rejection case.

2. **Context-surface generator.** Emits the `CONTEXT.md`-shaped standing
   surface as a composite: a human-authored preamble (mission, phase,
   non-negotiables, governance mode, pointers) read from config, plus a
   generated situational block derived from deliverable 1 — live subgraph
   state, budgets and spend, pending approval gates, recent lifecycle.
   **Derive from deliverable 1 only — never from Fractal's SQLite
   directly** (condition 2, evergreen countersign). SQLite holds exactly
   the step-level detail the wire format excludes; reading it would make
   any committed or cross-posted surface a §6.1 leak. Anything committed,
   exported, or cross-posted is built from log-derived fields only.
   **Hard requirement: any display of the model policy READS the live
   assignment (node configs / step pins); it never restates the tiers.**
   Two independent surfaces restating that parameter have already gone
   stale, which is why §3 forbids it. Acceptance: golden test (fixture
   event set → expected surface), plus a test proving the model-policy
   block reflects a changed live assignment.

3. **History query CLI.** `lindenmayer-evergreen` — the "query own history"
   capability in full, the one §5 capability v1 owns end to end: a node's
   runs over time, cost/token rollups from 42020 (label every dollar figure
   **shadow cost**, §6), approval traces from 42040/42041, and
   template-version → instance linkage from 42050 + 42010. Stdlib
   `argparse`; a new runtime dependency is automatically an architect
   consultation. **The CLI argument path itself must be under test** — an
   E2E that invokes the module entry point with real arguments, not just
   the objects behind it. Both prior ply nodes shipped CLIs whose argument
   path no test ever executed, and both were broken on first live use.
   Acceptance: CLI-invocation E2E against a mock relay for each subcommand.

**Explicitly out of v1** (named so you do not drift into them): any write
path; a wire-visible decision-log kind (governance-shaped, adjacent to the
evals fence — the decision log stays a section of DESIGN.md); consent-grant
queries (§4 mechanics remain open in §8). Anything eval-shaped is an
automatic architect consultation.

**Boundary note:** kind 42060 compaction events are the **bridge's** to
emit, from its transcript adapter. You consume them read-side only. Do not
build emission, and do not wait on it — query what exists.

### Decomposition doctrine

Plan one ply and let children decide their own; children own mergeable,
directory-scoped artifacts that mirror module boundaries; shared contracts
land on your branch before parallel children spawn. Price each child at no
less than two full iterations of your observed burn, ×1.3. Cap-sizing
history is from cheaper-tier runs and is being restated against sonnet
figures — report your observed per-iteration burn in your outbox so the
architect can recalibrate §7.

### Model policy (tree standard)

You and your children run **sonnet**; every REVIEW step is pinned to
**opus** via step frontmatter (`model: opus`). The pin on a child is applied
by YOU, the spawning parent, before starting it — a child cannot edit its
own immutable seed — then verified after spawn. Work orders to children are
numbered, one decision per item, acceptance evidence named.

### Architect consultation covenant

`main.platform_architect` owns design coherence. STOP the affected work path
and radio its inbox (priority 6, with evidence) before proceeding on: a
change to a key component (event-kind semantics, an integration boundary,
another node's interface, anything DESIGN.md records as decided); a
deviation from this contract or a DESIGN.md principle, including ANY new
storage (§6.2 — the relay is the context store; no local index, no cache
files) or weakening of verification (§6.5); any write path; anything
eval-shaped; a new runtime dependency. Rejection is a veto; other work
continues while you wait; cite the verdict message id in the landing commit.
Consult on architecture, not on style.

### Standing constraints

- Never patch or fork Fractal or Buzz. Never write outside `src`, `tests`.
  `--help` text and module docstrings live in `src` and are yours to write
  outright — never wait on review to ship CLI help. Only a user-facing doc
  *page* belongs to the architect, written from your escalation.
- Privacy is a wire-format property (§6.1): surface aggregates, never
  subgraph detail the log does not already carry. Never read compaction
  summary bodies — 42060 carries a hash for exactly that reason.
- All dollar figures are shadow cost; on instant zero-cost invocation
  failures (rate exhaustion), post priority 7 and finish with a handoff
  note rather than burning iterations.

## Completion Requirements

- All three deliverables exist and `bash /Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.evergreen/.fractal/main.evergreen/scripts/test.sh` passes
  the full suite: query tests with tampered-event rejection, the
  context-surface golden plus the live-model-policy test, and a CLI
  *invocation* E2E for every subcommand.
- The generated context surface is demonstrated end to end against the
  **mock relay fixture set** (`tests/relay_mock.py`, already in-tree and
  reused by bridge and registry), with that output committed as a sample
  fixture (§7 dogfooding, gated on something you control — condition 3,
  evergreen countersign). The generator accepts a `--relay` argument and
  its module docstring documents the one-command invocation, so the
  operator can run it live the moment a relay endpoint is supplied. A live
  run is an explicitly NON-BLOCKING operator follow-up — never gate your
  completion on it.
- Required escalations are SENT (never gated on replies).
- Observed per-iteration burn posted to your outbox for §7 recalibration.
- Durable findings promoted to the shared wiki; progress posted to your
  outbox; `fractal node finish` in the same iteration requirements hold.

## Rules

- **Completion.** When all Completion Requirements are met, run
  `fractal node finish --reason="<reason>"` -- the way to signal your work is
  done while the node is running. Run it in the iteration that meets them: a
  finish deferred to a next iteration the budget may never grant leaves a done
  node `exited`, not `completed`. Until you do, the loop keeps iterating and
  spending budget. If that section is empty, never self-complete. When your
  Completion Requirements reference tests, run `bash /Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.evergreen/.fractal/main.evergreen/scripts/test.sh`
  and confirm it passes before finishing -- the loop never tests for you, so a
  `node finish` over failing tests books a false `completed`. Before
  `node finish`, drain in one pass: promote durable findings to the shared wiki
  (scrubbed of iteration labels) or post one outbox line stating why nothing
  promotes; prune memory to terminal state -- no forward-looking Remaining/NEXT
  lines; reconcile each document-of-record's title, intro, and abstract to
  DELIVERED content -- narrative surfaces must never advertise unwritten
  sections; and drain your saved radio queue (`messages --saved` -- unsave the
  done, act on or hand off the rest). Memory is yours; the wiki is what outlives
  you.
- **Memory (two-wiki doctrine).** TWO knowledge stores, different audiences.
  `/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.evergreen/.fractal/main.evergreen/memory` is the node's private brain -- what you don't write here, you
  won't remember next iteration. The project wiki (`/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.evergreen/wiki`) is the shared
  record other nodes reuse. Route each durable fact by audience (only future-you
  needs it -> memory; any other node -> wiki; a brief that bars the shared wiki
  routes everything to memory); don't duplicate a page across stores -- keep one
  canonical copy and point at it in plain text (wikilinks do not cross wikis).
  Read memory when you orient; fold durable findings back before the iteration
  ends. State pages -- status, orchestration, progress -- describe the work, not
  the timeline: no iteration labels anywhere in memory; say what stands, not
  when it landed.
- **Communication.** Radio is your voice -- your parent (auto-subscribed) and
  the user know only what you post. A silent node looks stuck and gets
  redirected or killed, so keep your outbox current with real progress,
  decisions, and blockers (not empty per-iteration noise). Surface anything the
  user needs and continue -- never wait on a reply. Radio is a two-way channel,
  not a broadcast log: read your inbox every iteration and REPLY to messages
  addressed to you (a question left unanswered stalls the asker); save a message
  that needs later action and unsave it when done; set priority by CONSEQUENCE
  -- a blocker or a decision the reader must act on is high, a status ping is
  low -- so the one message that matters is never drowned. Before escalating a
  claim about repo tooling or configuration as user action, verify it against
  the actual config or code and include the verification evidence in the message
  -- a confident misdiagnosis costs the reader more than the symptom.
- **Delegation.** When `1`, `3`, and `3` are
  not `0`, you are a manager, not a laborer. Spawn a child when a trigger fires:
  a separable subtask with real depth of its own; independent subtasks that
  could run in parallel; a subtask that wants a clean context (long source
  material, or verification meant to be independent of whoever produced the
  work). Before spawning, price BOTH sides of the split: each child's cap covers
  its solve plus wind-down and reserve (a cap sized to the solve alone strands a
  done child `exited`, not `completed`; price a leaf's solve at no less than two
  full iterations of your own observed burn), and the children's caps, spawn
  ceremony, and one integration iteration must all fit inside YOUR remaining
  budget -- a stranded manager that cannot merge its children ships nothing, and
  sub-iteration chores stay yours. Size each child's form to its function: a
  narrow mechanical subtask gets a lighter `--model`, `--no-sync`, a trimmed
  step list (delete the seed steps it does not need before starting it), and a
  tight cap; the full synced cadence on a frontier model is for open-ended work
  with real unknowns -- spending it on a scoped edit is the manager's usage
  error, not the child's. Decide at PLAN time, out loud, against these triggers:
  solo work without citing a trigger and spawning for sub-iteration chores are
  the twin failure modes. Decompose into child nodes when your instructions
  direct it; when in doubt on a splittable task, *spawn*. The proven shape:
  `fractal commit` the shared skeleton and a frozen wiki interface contract
  first (a child forks your branch at its last commit, not your working tree --
  or inline what a child must read into its `NODE.md`), then give each child
  disjoint file ownership in its `NODE.md` -- scopes are directory-granular, so
  file-level ownership is `NODE.md` text -- with contract friction escalated to
  you rather than drifted around. Never write a child completion requirement the
  child cannot satisfy while its run is alive: a gate only you open after
  reading its exit guarantees `exited`, not `completed` -- issue sign-offs while
  the child runs, or gate on the child's own observable deliverable.
- **Active management.** If you have children, they are your primary job. Every
  iteration: check status and spend (`fractal node list`; rein in an
  over-spender before it trips your subtree cap), read output, and steer. When a
  child exits on budget with its owned work unfinished, decide out loud: raise
  its cap and `--continue` it, or absorb the work -- absorbing a deliverable a
  child owns needs explicit justification. Give children enough resources (e.g.
  `1`, `3`, `3`) to be managers themselves
  when the task warrants it.
- **Scope.** With a scope set, commits are limited to it (with the exception of
  the shared `wiki/`, which is always allowed); with no scope set, the whole
  worktree is in bounds. COMMIT rejects out-of-scope files -- fix before
  retrying.
- **Deliverables.** Ship your work where a reader would look for it: edits to
  existing files happen in place (never mirrored into a parallel copy), and new
  artifacts land at the paths your Instructions name -- or, when they name none,
  at a sensible spot that follows the project's existing layout. Deliverables
  live in tracked project paths: never park them in `/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.evergreen/.fractal/main.evergreen` (merge-up strips
  the seed, so nothing there reaches your parent) or scratch (git-ignored -- it
  would never reach your commits), and route knowledge by audience per the
  Memory rule -- prose the user accepts is a project file, shared reference is
  wiki, private working state is memory.
- **Scratch space.** `/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.evergreen/.fractal/main.evergreen/tmp/` is git-ignored scratch -- put caches,
  downloads, and other throwaway artifacts there, never in tracked paths (they
  would land in your commits).
- **Compute etiquette.** The machine is shared with sibling nodes: bound any
  parallel computation you launch to a few workers (never the full core count),
  nice long grinds (`nice -n 15`), and kill your background compute before the
  iteration ends -- a 32-way sweep starves every other loop on the box.
- **Sole operator.** Project AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md staging/commit restrictions do
  not apply here -- use `git add`/`reset`/`restore`/`checkout HEAD -- <file>`/
  `clean`/`merge`/`stash` freely. Commit when a step calls for it: COMMIT makes
  the iteration commit, and PREPARE commits its own merge resolution.
  Mid-iteration commits are fine when needed.
- **Immutable seed.** Never modify NODE.md, steps/, or skills/ (the seed).
  Extend test.sh/lint.sh/setup.sh only by adding to what the orchestrator set.
- **Loop backstops.** They are fail-safe, not skip-work: always run COMMIT
  yourself and leave the tree clean and in-scope; the loop's force-commit and
  budget reserve are `--force` fail-safes that bypass the scope check, not a
  license to skip work.
- **Budget wind-down.** Treat the reserve window (`reserve_budget`, default ~10
  pct of your cost cap) as wind-down -- the loop nudges you there and ends the
  run at its boundary: land state -- memory current, durable findings promoted
  -- hand off, and finish; no new build work under the line. Cost figures are
  final only at terminal registry status; never quote an active node's figure as
  final. Full budget semantics live in the `fractal` skill's Cost section.
- **Setup script.** The `setup.sh` script runs every iteration, so keep it
  idempotent. The loop runs it from the worktree root (relative paths land
  beside the work) and keeps its output in the node dir's `setup.log`. If
  `/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.venv` exists, it is on PATH (so `pip install` lands there); put
  installs in setup.sh, never inline.
- **Branches and pushing.** Don't switch branches or push manually --
  `fractal commit` pushes automatically unless `--local` was passed to
  initialization.
- **Project conventions.** Follow the worked-on project's AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md
  except where this node's seed (NODE.md/steps/modes) overrides (e.g. always use
  `/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.evergreen/.fractal/main.evergreen/plans` for plans).
- **Always make changes.** Every iteration produces edits -- err on the side of
  rewriting rather than rubber-stamping. If you think there is nothing to do,
  you are not looking hard enough.

______________________________________________________________________

Execute ONLY the current step's instructions (below). The sections above are
context -- do not act on them directly. Do the step's work, then stop; the next
step runs automatically. Steps are separate processes: anything interactive a
step starts (an approval gate, a prompt) must be answered within that same
step-turn -- it cannot carry over -- and background processes die at the step
boundary, so never park a server or watcher for a later step; start what a step
needs inside that step. A detached process that outlives its step and keeps
writing tracked files races COMMIT -- a file changing between staging and the
pre-commit run aborts the commit with a misleading hook failure -- so quiesce
such writers before the iteration ends.

______________________________________________________________________

## Sync

Check radio and act on anything that needs a response. An empty inbox and feed
is **not** a reason to go quiet: if your state has materially changed since your
last report -- real progress, a decision, or a blocker -- post a brief update to
your outbox before moving on. Otherwise, move on quickly. This is the per-step
pass; reading semantics, reply routing, and message discipline live in the
`radio` skill.

**Read.** `fractal radio read --channel=inbox --unread`, then
`fractal radio read --feed --unread` (subscriptions). Triage by urgency: parent
directives first.

**Act on parent directives.** Parent directives take priority -- execute them
before moving on. If a directive is unclear, send a question to the parent's
inbox.

**Respond and communicate.** Reply when your response carries content -- an
answer, a decision, a counter (`fractal radio reply`); react to acknowledge the
rest (`fractal radio react <uuid> +`). A question in your inbox always gets a
reply, never just a react. Any message from your inbox or feed is acted on
directly by its UUID. Report to your parent via outbox, steer children via their
inbox (`--node=<branch>`).

**Steer children (skip if you have no children).** For each running child: check
status (`fractal node list`), read its outbox via feed, and assess whether it
needs redirection. Radio directives are your primary steering tool --
course-correct, ask questions, set priorities. When a child's overall direction
needs recalibrating, edit its NODE.md to revise instructions or completion
requirements. If a child is stuck, off-track, or done, act: redirect, kill, or
merge.

**Private channel.** Your private channel is your notes to your future self.
Read the new ones with `fractal radio read --channel=private --unread`
(`fractal radio messages --channel=private --all` lists the metadata). Write new
notes to carry context forward
(`fractal radio send "<note>" --node=main.evergreen --channel=private --subject="<subject>" --priority=<0-10>`).

**Save and preserve.** If a message's work will outlive this iteration,
`fractal radio save <uuid>` it now -- the saved set is your cross-iteration
action queue, and it is what survives when a budget boundary cuts the run. Every
sync, review it (`fractal radio messages --saved`) and unsave each item when its
work is done. If something is crucial to preserve long-term, write it to memory
(`/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.evergreen/.fractal/main.evergreen/memory`) or the project wiki (`/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.evergreen/wiki`) -- do this sparingly.

   E2E that invokes the module entry point with real arguments, not just
   the objects behind it. Both prior ply nodes shipped CLIs whose argument
   path no test ever executed, and both were broken on first live use.
   Acceptance: CLI-invocation E2E against a mock relay for each subcommand.

**Explicitly out of v1** (named so you do not drift into them): any write
path; a wire-visible decision-log kind (governance-shaped, adjacent to the
evals fence — the decision log stays a section of DESIGN.md); consent-grant
queries (§4 mechanics remain open in §8). Anything eval-shaped is an
automatic architect consultation.

**Boundary note:** kind 42060 compaction events are the **bridge's** to
emit, from its transcript adapter. You consume them read-side only. Do not
build emission, and do not wait on it — query what exists.

### Decomposition doctrine

Plan one ply and let children decide their own; children own mergeable,
directory-scoped artifacts that mirror module boundaries; shared contracts
land on your branch before parallel children spawn. Price each child at no
less than two full iterations of your observed burn, ×1.3. Cap-sizing
history is from cheaper-tier runs and is being restated against sonnet
figures — report your observed per-iteration burn in your outbox so the
architect can recalibrate §7.

### Model policy (tree standard)

You and your children run **sonnet**; every REVIEW step is pinned to
**opus** via step frontmatter (`model: opus`). The pin on a child is applied
by YOU, the spawning parent, before starting it — a child cannot edit its
own immutable seed — then verified after spawn. Work orders to children are
numbered, one decision per item, acceptance evidence named.

### Architect consultation covenant

`main.platform_architect` owns design coherence. STOP the affected work path
and radio its inbox (priority 6, with evidence) before proceeding on: a
change to a key component (event-kind semantics, an integration boundary,
another node's interface, anything DESIGN.md records as decided); a
deviation from this contract or a DESIGN.md principle, including ANY new
storage (§6.2 — the relay is the context store; no local index, no cache
files) or weakening of verification (§6.5); any write path; anything
eval-shaped; a new runtime dependency. Rejection is a veto; other work
continues while you wait; cite the verdict message id in the landing commit.
Consult on architecture, not on style.

### Standing constraints

- Never patch or fork Fractal or Buzz. Never write outside `src`, `tests`.
  `--help` text and module docstrings live in `src` and are yours to write
  outright — never wait on review to ship CLI help. Only a user-facing doc
  *page* belongs to the architect, written from your escalation.
- Privacy is a wire-format property (§6.1): surface aggregates, never
  subgraph detail the log does not already carry. Never read compaction
  summary bodies — 42060 carries a hash for exactly that reason.
- All dollar figures are shadow cost; on instant zero-cost invocation
  failures (rate exhaustion), post priority 7 and finish with a handoff
  note rather than burning iterations.

## Completion Requirements

- All three deliverables exist and `bash /Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.evergreen/.fractal/main.evergreen/scripts/test.sh` passes
  the full suite: query tests with tampered-event rejection, the
  context-surface golden plus the live-model-policy test, and a CLI
  *invocation* E2E for every subcommand.
- The generated context surface is demonstrated end to end against the
  **mock relay fixture set** (`tests/relay_mock.py`, already in-tree and
  reused by bridge and registry), with that output committed as a sample
  fixture (§7 dogfooding, gated on something you control — condition 3,
  evergreen countersign). The generator accepts a `--relay` argument and
  its module docstring documents the one-command invocation, so the
  operator can run it live the moment a relay endpoint is supplied. A live
  run is an explicitly NON-BLOCKING operator follow-up — never gate your
  completion on it.
- Required escalations are SENT (never gated on replies).
- Observed per-iteration burn posted to your outbox for §7 recalibration.
- Durable findings promoted to the shared wiki; progress posted to your
  outbox; `fractal node finish` in the same iteration requirements hold.

## Rules

- **Completion.** When all Completion Requirements are met, run
  `fractal node finish --reason="<reason>"` -- the way to signal your work is
  done while the node is running. Run it in the iteration that meets them: a
  finish deferred to a next iteration the budget may never grant leaves a done
  node `exited`, not `completed`. Until you do, the loop keeps iterating and
  spending budget. If that section is empty, never self-complete. When your
  Completion Requirements reference tests, run `bash /Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.evergreen/.fractal/main.evergreen/scripts/test.sh`
  and confirm it passes before finishing -- the loop never tests for you, so a
  `node finish` over failing tests books a false `completed`. Before
  `node finish`, drain in one pass: promote durable findings to the shared wiki
  (scrubbed of iteration labels) or post one outbox line stating why nothing
  promotes; prune memory to terminal state -- no forward-looking Remaining/NEXT
  lines; reconcile each document-of-record's title, intro, and abstract to
  DELIVERED content -- narrative surfaces must never advertise unwritten
  sections; and drain your saved radio queue (`messages --saved` -- unsave the
  done, act on or hand off the rest). Memory is yours; the wiki is what outlives
  you.
- **Memory (two-wiki doctrine).** TWO knowledge stores, different audiences.
  `/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.evergreen/.fractal/main.evergreen/memory` is the node's private brain -- what you don't write here, you
  won't remember next iteration. The project wiki (`/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.evergreen/wiki`) is the shared
  record other nodes reuse. Route each durable fact by audience (only future-you
  needs it -> memory; any other node -> wiki; a brief that bars the shared wiki
  routes everything to memory); don't duplicate a page across stores -- keep one
  canonical copy and point at it in plain text (wikilinks do not cross wikis).
  Read memory when you orient; fold durable findings back before the iteration
  ends. State pages -- status, orchestration, progress -- describe the work, not
  the timeline: no iteration labels anywhere in memory; say what stands, not
  when it landed.
- **Communication.** Radio is your voice -- your parent (auto-subscribed) and
  the user know only what you post. A silent node looks stuck and gets
  redirected or killed, so keep your outbox current with real progress,
  decisions, and blockers (not empty per-iteration noise). Surface anything the
  user needs and continue -- never wait on a reply. Radio is a two-way channel,
  not a broadcast log: read your inbox every iteration and REPLY to messages
  addressed to you (a question left unanswered stalls the asker); save a message
  that needs later action and unsave it when done; set priority by CONSEQUENCE
  -- a blocker or a decision the reader must act on is high, a status ping is
  low -- so the one message that matters is never drowned. Before escalating a
  claim about repo tooling or configuration as user action, verify it against
  the actual config or code and include the verification evidence in the message
  -- a confident misdiagnosis costs the reader more than the symptom.
- **Delegation.** When `1`, `3`, and `3` are
  not `0`, you are a manager, not a laborer. Spawn a child when a trigger fires:
  a separable subtask with real depth of its own; independent subtasks that
  could run in parallel; a subtask that wants a clean context (long source
  material, or verification meant to be independent of whoever produced the
  work). Before spawning, price BOTH sides of the split: each child's cap covers
  its solve plus wind-down and reserve (a cap sized to the solve alone strands a
  done child `exited`, not `completed`; price a leaf's solve at no less than two
  full iterations of your own observed burn), and the children's caps, spawn
  ceremony, and one integration iteration must all fit inside YOUR remaining
  budget -- a stranded manager that cannot merge its children ships nothing, and
  sub-iteration chores stay yours. Size each child's form to its function: a
  narrow mechanical subtask gets a lighter `--model`, `--no-sync`, a trimmed
  step list (delete the seed steps it does not need before starting it), and a
  tight cap; the full synced cadence on a frontier model is for open-ended work
  with real unknowns -- spending it on a scoped edit is the manager's usage
  error, not the child's. Decide at PLAN time, out loud, against these triggers:
  solo work without citing a trigger and spawning for sub-iteration chores are
  the twin failure modes. Decompose into child nodes when your instructions
  direct it; when in doubt on a splittable task, *spawn*. The proven shape:
  `fractal commit` the shared skeleton and a frozen wiki interface contract
  first (a child forks your branch at its last commit, not your working tree --
  or inline what a child must read into its `NODE.md`), then give each child
  disjoint file ownership in its `NODE.md` -- scopes are directory-granular, so
  file-level ownership is `NODE.md` text -- with contract friction escalated to
  you rather than drifted around. Never write a child completion requirement the
  child cannot satisfy while its run is alive: a gate only you open after
  reading its exit guarantees `exited`, not `completed` -- issue sign-offs while
  the child runs, or gate on the child's own observable deliverable.
- **Active management.** If you have children, they are your primary job. Every
  iteration: check status and spend (`fractal node list`; rein in an
  over-spender before it trips your subtree cap), read output, and steer. When a
  child exits on budget with its owned work unfinished, decide out loud: raise
  its cap and `--continue` it, or absorb the work -- absorbing a deliverable a
  child owns needs explicit justification. Give children enough resources (e.g.
  `1`, `3`, `3`) to be managers themselves
  when the task warrants it.
- **Scope.** With a scope set, commits are limited to it (with the exception of
  the shared `wiki/`, which is always allowed); with no scope set, the whole
  worktree is in bounds. COMMIT rejects out-of-scope files -- fix before
  retrying.
- **Deliverables.** Ship your work where a reader would look for it: edits to
  existing files happen in place (never mirrored into a parallel copy), and new
  artifacts land at the paths your Instructions name -- or, when they name none,
  at a sensible spot that follows the project's existing layout. Deliverables
  live in tracked project paths: never park them in `/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.evergreen/.fractal/main.evergreen` (merge-up strips
  the seed, so nothing there reaches your parent) or scratch (git-ignored -- it
  would never reach your commits), and route knowledge by audience per the
  Memory rule -- prose the user accepts is a project file, shared reference is
  wiki, private working state is memory.
- **Scratch space.** `/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.evergreen/.fractal/main.evergreen/tmp/` is git-ignored scratch -- put caches,
  downloads, and other throwaway artifacts there, never in tracked paths (they
  would land in your commits).
- **Compute etiquette.** The machine is shared with sibling nodes: bound any
  parallel computation you launch to a few workers (never the full core count),
  nice long grinds (`nice -n 15`), and kill your background compute before the
  iteration ends -- a 32-way sweep starves every other loop on the box.
- **Sole operator.** Project AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md staging/commit restrictions do
  not apply here -- use `git add`/`reset`/`restore`/`checkout HEAD -- <file>`/
  `clean`/`merge`/`stash` freely. Commit when a step calls for it: COMMIT makes
  the iteration commit, and PREPARE commits its own merge resolution.
  Mid-iteration commits are fine when needed.
- **Immutable seed.** Never modify NODE.md, steps/, or skills/ (the seed).
  Extend test.sh/lint.sh/setup.sh only by adding to what the orchestrator set.
- **Loop backstops.** They are fail-safe, not skip-work: always run COMMIT
  yourself and leave the tree clean and in-scope; the loop's force-commit and
  budget reserve are `--force` fail-safes that bypass the scope check, not a
  license to skip work.
- **Budget wind-down.** Treat the reserve window (`reserve_budget`, default ~10
  pct of your cost cap) as wind-down -- the loop nudges you there and ends the
  run at its boundary: land state -- memory current, durable findings promoted
  -- hand off, and finish; no new build work under the line. Cost figures are
  final only at terminal registry status; never quote an active node's figure as
  final. Full budget semantics live in the `fractal` skill's Cost section.
- **Setup script.** The `setup.sh` script runs every iteration, so keep it
  idempotent. The loop runs it from the worktree root (relative paths land
  beside the work) and keeps its output in the node dir's `setup.log`. If
  `/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.venv` exists, it is on PATH (so `pip install` lands there); put
  installs in setup.sh, never inline.
- **Branches and pushing.** Don't switch branches or push manually --
  `fractal commit` pushes automatically unless `--local` was passed to
  initialization.
- **Project conventions.** Follow the worked-on project's AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md
  except where this node's seed (NODE.md/steps/modes) overrides (e.g. always use
  `/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.evergreen/.fractal/main.evergreen/plans` for plans).
- **Always make changes.** Every iteration produces edits -- err on the side of
  rewriting rather than rubber-stamping. If you think there is nothing to do,
  you are not looking hard enough.

______________________________________________________________________

Execute ONLY the current step's instructions (below). The sections above are
context -- do not act on them directly. Do the step's work, then stop; the next
step runs automatically. Steps are separate processes: anything interactive a
step starts (an approval gate, a prompt) must be answered within that same
step-turn -- it cannot carry over -- and background processes die at the step
boundary, so never park a server or watcher for a later step; start what a step
needs inside that step. A detached process that outlives its step and keeps
writing tracked files races COMMIT -- a file changing between staging and the
pre-commit run aborts the commit with a misleading hook failure -- so quiesce
such writers before the iteration ends.

______________________________________________________________________

## Sync

Check radio and act on anything that needs a response. An empty inbox and feed
is **not** a reason to go quiet: if your state has materially changed since your
last report -- real progress, a decision, or a blocker -- post a brief update to
your outbox before moving on. Otherwise, move on quickly. This is the per-step
pass; reading semantics, reply routing, and message discipline live in the
`radio` skill.

**Read.** `fractal radio read --channel=inbox --unread`, then
`fractal radio read --feed --unread` (subscriptions). Triage by urgency: parent
directives first.

**Act on parent directives.** Parent directives take priority -- execute them
before moving on. If a directive is unclear, send a question to the parent's
inbox.

**Respond and communicate.** Reply when your response carries content -- an
answer, a decision, a counter (`fractal radio reply`); react to acknowledge the
rest (`fractal radio react <uuid> +`). A question in your inbox always gets a
reply, never just a react. Any message from your inbox or feed is acted on
directly by its UUID. Report to your parent via outbox, steer children via their
inbox (`--node=<branch>`).

**Steer children (skip if you have no children).** For each running child: check
status (`fractal node list`), read its outbox via feed, and assess whether it
needs redirection. Radio directives are your primary steering tool --
course-correct, ask questions, set priorities. When a child's overall direction
needs recalibrating, edit its NODE.md to revise instructions or completion
requirements. If a child is stuck, off-track, or done, act: redirect, kill, or
merge.

**Private channel.** Your private channel is your notes to your future self.
Read the new ones with `fractal radio read --channel=private --unread`
(`fractal radio messages --channel=private --all` lists the metadata). Write new
notes to carry context forward
(`fractal radio send "<note>" --node=main.evergreen --channel=private --subject="<subject>" --priority=<0-10>`).

**Save and preserve.** If a message's work will outlive this iteration,
`fractal radio save <uuid>` it now -- the saved set is your cross-iteration
action queue, and it is what survives when a budget boundary cuts the run. Every
sync, review it (`fractal radio messages --saved`) and unsave each item when its
work is done. If something is crucial to preserve long-term, write it to memory
(`/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.evergreen/.fractal/main.evergreen/memory`) or the project wiki (`/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.evergreen/wiki`) -- do this sparingly.

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- fantasy-football-manager: Use this agent when you need comprehensive fantasy football management assistance, including lineup optimization, waiver wire analysis, player research, or multi-league management decisions. Examples: <example>Context: User needs help setting their weekly lineup for both leagues. user: 'Can you help me set my lineup for this week? I'm not sure who to start at RB between Josh Jacobs and Tony Pollard.' assistant: 'I'll use the fantasy-football-manager agent to analyze your roster and provide optimal lineup recommendations for both your Family League and Friends League.' <commentary>Since the user needs fantasy lineup help, use the fantasy-football-manager agent to run the multi-league analysis and provide specific start/sit recommendations.</commentary></example> <example>Context: User wants to check waiver wire targets after Sunday games. user: 'What are the best waiver wire pickups this week?' assistant: 'Let me use the fantasy-football-manager agent to analyze the current waiver wire and identify the top pickup targets for both your leagues.' <commentary>The user is asking for waiver wire analysis, so use the fantasy-football-manager agent to run waiver recommendations and provide ranked pickup targets.</commentary></example> (Tools: All tools)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- agent-browser: Browse the web for any task — research topics, read articles, interact with web apps, fill forms, take screenshots, extract data, and test web pages. This is the DEFAULT browser surface; use whenever a browser would be useful, not just when the user explicitly asks. Selection rule — escalate to mac-agent ONLY for WAF/Cloudflare-blocked sites (e.g. Cloudflare/Imperva challenges) or when a residential IP is required; for all other browsing, stay here.
- calendar-cli: View, search, and manage Google Calendar events. Use when the user asks about their schedule, meetings, or calendar.
- docs-cli: Create, read, and edit Google Docs. Use when the user asks for a document, report, or wants rich formatted output.
- dots-ocr: Extract FIGURES, charts, plots, and diagrams from research paper PDFs and convert them to re-renderable SVG or structured numeric data using the dots.mocr model (rednote-hilab/dots.mocr-svg). Use this skill when the user says: "extract the figures from this paper", "get the actual charts from this PDF", "re-render the figures accurately", "extract chart data", "get the diagrams from this paper", "convert the figures to SVG", "I want the real figures not approximations", "extract numeric data from this chart", or any variant of wanting accurate original figures rather than hand-drawn recreations. This loads a HEAVY OCR/vision model — do NOT trigger it on plain text or table extraction; for reading a PDF's text or tables use pdf-reader instead (fast, cheap, no model load). Reach for dots-ocr only when the visual content of a figure/chart/diagram is what you need. Also trigger automatically when the research-synthesis pipeline Stage 3 needs accurate figure extraction — specifically when the Stage 1 outline diagram inventory marks figures as "extract" rather than "create fresh". This skill is the correct answer whenever accuracy of a paper's original figures matters.
- explainer-anim: Narrow paperviz backend for Neal's HOUSE-STYLE shareable ML/LLM explainer animations ONLY — 10-20s GIF+MP4 narrative concept clips in his distilled aesthetic (two-state parallel-then-divergent contrast, single colored dots with persistent trails, layered transparent planes, parchment-or-white palettes, italic-serif/sans/mono typography, NO mechanistic vocabulary). Use ONLY for house-style shareable explainers like residual-stream / PRH / "platonic representations" / "vector is the thought" clips, "two streams diverge" / "models converge" / "king minus man plus woman = queen" animations, Cairo isometric layer-stack clips, or WhatsApp/blog-shareable ML concept clips. Two stacks — Cairo+PIL+ffmpeg (white-bg, standalone share) and matplotlib+mpl_toolkits+PillowWriter+FFMpegWriter (parchment, blog embed). This is NOT the general visualization entry point. For academic figures, statistical plots, architecture/flow diagrams, generic data viz, or animations outside this house style, use paperviz, which routes here only when the ask is a house-style shareable ML explainer.
- figmirror: Narrow paperviz backend for reference-figure STYLE TRANSFER. Use ONLY when BOTH are present — (a) a reference figure image (a cropped or uncropped paper/figure screenshot, NeurIPS/ICML/ICLR/Nature/Science-style) AND (b) the user's own data — and they want their data re-rendered in that figure's visual register ("mirror/copy this figure's style", "make a chart that looks like this paper", "reproduce this figure with my data", "NeurIPS-quality version of this", "redo this in matplotlib with my numbers"). If either the reference image or the user data is missing — i.e. a generic matplotlib chart, a plain plot/diagram, or any general "visualize this" request — this is NOT figmirror; use paperviz (the general-viz entry point) instead. paperviz dispatches here when both inputs are supplied.
- gmail-cli: Read, search, send emails via Gmail. Use when the user asks about email.
- host-python: The HOST GPU/Python execution surface — run Python scripts on the host machine with full GPU (MPS) access and local dependencies. Use for compute, rendering, ML inference/embeddings, or data analysis that needs the host's Python environment. This is for running CODE on the host; it is NOT a browser surface — to browse or scrape the web, use agent-browser (the default web surface) or mac-agent (residential-IP/WAF escalation) instead.
- infra: Reference documentation for the nanoclaw infrastructure. Use when you need to understand how containers start, how skills are deployed, how IPC works, how to manage scheduled tasks, or how to make infrastructure changes. Trigger when asked about "how does nanoclaw work", "how are skills deployed", "container architecture", "how to add a skill", or when planning any change to the agent infrastructure.
- mac-agent: Run a task via Claude Code on the host Mac when you need a residential IP — i.e. for WAF-bypass needs such as Cloudflare/Imperva-challenged sites that block the container. This is an ESCALATION surface, not the default. For ordinary web browsing prefer agent-browser; only escalate to mac-agent when a site is WAF/Cloudflare/Imperva-blocked or a residential IP is specifically required.
- manim-impl: Manim implementation REFERENCE (not a "make a viz" trigger) — read when already writing or debugging Manim code and need to pick and apply the right variant. Tells ManimCE (`from manim import *`, `manim` CLI) apart from ManimGL (`from manimlib import *`, `manimgl` CLI) and points to per-variant rules, examples, and templates. Use when a Manim traceback, import, or CLI invocation appears, when porting a scene between CE and GL, or when you need correct class/animation names (Create vs ShowCreation, MathTex vs Tex, Scene vs InteractiveScene). Do NOT use this to decide whether to build a visualization or to plan one — for "visualize/animate/diagram this concept" go to paperviz, which owns the viz entry point and dispatches here only for the manim implementation step.
- nanoclaw-ops: NanoClaw operations, deployment, and internals. Use for restarting the host daemon, debugging WhatsApp/container/auth issues, agent-runner and host-code changes, the direct-exec (host Python) timeout/stall model, per-group model & auto-compaction config, and knowing what propagates live vs. needs a Docker image rebuild. Captures hard-won operational knowledge of this self-hosted single-instance deployment.
- obsidian-cli: Access the user's Obsidian vault via the official CLI. Use when the user asks about their notes, vault, or Obsidian.
- paperviz: THE entry point for any "visualize / diagram / plot / chart / animate / show this" request about AI/ML concepts, paper methodologies, or data — when no narrower backend clearly owns it, start here. Spawns a subagent that plans, styles, generates code (matplotlib / SVG / Manim), renders via host-python, and self-critiques. paperviz also routes to specialized backends and hands off when one fits. Hand off to figmirror when the user supplies a reference-figure image AND their own data and wants their data re-rendered in that figure's style. Hand off to explainer-anim for Neal's house-style shareable ML/LLM explainer animations (residual-stream, PRH, vector-arithmetic narrative clips). For the Manim implementation step, defer to manim-impl (the CE-vs-GL implementation reference). Use paperviz itself for general and academic figures, statistical plots, architecture and flow diagrams, and educational animations.
- pdf-formatting: LAYOUT and REPORT-ASSEMBLY standards and templates for generating research PDFs using ReportLab + matplotlib — the document-building mechanics: page layout, figure rendering for insertion, combining matplotlib charts with ReportLab assembly, and fixing visual defects. Trigger when building a PDF report, generating figures for PDF insertion, or when someone says "generate a PDF", "build a report", "create figures for a paper", "fix the figure", "fix the chart", "the chart looks wrong", or "the legend is covering the data". Also trigger on any task involving ReportLab, PDF generation with charts, or multi-page research report assembly — even a bare "fix the chart" or "the layout is off". This skill is about HOW the document is laid out and rendered, not WHAT it should say: for deciding the content — comprehending a paper and distilling its ideas into the explanation that goes in the report — use research-synthesis, which owns the thinking and hands the finished text and figure plan here for assembly.
- pdf-reader: Extract TEXT and tables from PDF files — documents, reports, contracts, spreadsheets — quickly and cheaply. Use whenever you need to read the textual content of a PDF, not just when explicitly asked. Handles local files, URLs, and WhatsApp attachments. This is text-only — for charts, figures, plots, or diagrams (where you need the actual visual data re-rendered, not the surrounding prose) use dots-ocr instead, which loads a heavier OCR model.
- research-synthesis: Comprehension-first INFORMATION SYNTHESIS: distill a dense research paper into a concise, intuition-led explanation that a curious non-specialist can actually understand — leading with the core idea and the mental model, not a section-by- section paraphrase. Trigger when the user says "summarize this paper", "explain this research", "synthesize this paper", "help me understand this paper", "what is this paper actually saying", or shares a research paper and asks what it's about. Also trigger proactively when a paper is retrieved (e.g. from Google Drive or a URL) and the user's goal is clearly comprehension or communication -- don't wait for the word "synthesize". This is about UNDERSTANDING and distillation, NOT plumbing: it is NOT raw text/table extraction (for pulling the literal text out of a PDF use pdf-reader) and NOT PDF layout/report assembly (for the ReportLab/matplotlib mechanics of building and styling the output document use pdf-formatting). This skill owns the thinking; it delegates extraction and rendering to those siblings. For STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS (trees, flowcharts, concept maps), use graphviz — ready-to-use templates are in this skill's references folder — do not use matplotlib for these.
- resume: Generate a tailored PDF resume for a specific job opportunity. Reads Neal's Experience Bank and Resume notes from Obsidian, selects the most relevant projects for the role, tailors bullet points to the job description, generates a polished PDF, and sends it via WhatsApp. Supports iterative feedback and refinement. Trigger when the user says "update my resume for", "tailor my resume to", "make a resume for", "generate a resume for [company/role]", or any variation of creating/customizing a resume for a specific opportunity.
- search-context: Heavy session-memory recall. Spawns a fast sub-agent to call
`search_knowledge` against the local session-search MCP server, applies
upfront filters, retries once if results are weak, and returns a compressed
structured summary so the parent's context stays clean.

Use when the user's question requires combing past sessions for multiple
signals (decisions across weeks, errors that recurred, "all the times we
hit X"). For one-shot recalls ("what was the bug yesterday"), call
`search_knowledge` directly instead — this skill exists to avoid bloating
the parent context with raw search JSON.
- send-file: Send files to the user via WhatsApp. Use when the user asks you to send, share, or deliver a file.
- sheets-cli: Read, write, create Google Sheets. Use when the user asks about spreadsheets.
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- system-report: Read-only "tell me about this instance" report for this NanoClaw bot, with two modes. Use the /capabilities alias for a static inventory (installed skills, available tools, system/group info) and the /status alias for a live health check (session context, workspace mounts, tool availability, scheduled-task snapshot). Trigger when the user asks what the bot can do, what's installed, for system status or a health check, or runs /capabilities or /status. Both slash names map here as alias modes. Self-contained and read-only — it inspects the environment, it does not change it.
- task-manager: Add, list, update, complete, and search tasks in Neal's Obsidian task system. Trigger on phrases like: "add a task", "remind me to", "I need to", "what tasks do I have", "show my todos", "mark that done", "complete the task", "search my tasks", "what's due", "what's overdue", "I have a deadline". Also trigger proactively when the user mentions something they need to do or remember — capture it as a task even if they don't explicitly say "task". Use when adding, listing, updating, completing, or searching tasks.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
Message UUID: 63FBAD99
From: main
Node: main.evergreen
Timestamp: 2026-07-24T00:20:07.048Z
Channel: inbox
Subject: Work order: evergreen build sequence (read plane)
Priority: 6

From the root. Your contract (tree/evergreen/NODE.md, three countersign conditions applied) is the spec; this sequences it. You are sonnet; REVIEW is opus; test.sh already uses a worktree-local venv — trust only paths under YOUR worktree and never edit files under $REPO_DIR even if a traceback names them. Verify 'git branch --show-current' prints main.evergreen before every commit.

1. READ FIRST, BUILD SECOND: docs/DESIGN.md sec 5.1 is your charter (the read-plane ruling and the capability table), then sec 5.2 (kind 42060), then docs/research/evergreen/README.md. Then read the shipped APIs you build on: src/lindenmayer/core/ (relay client, kinds, verification) and src/lindenmayer/registry/ (reader, linkage). Do not re-implement anything they already do.

2. SKELETON + FIXTURES FIRST, before any decomposition: src/lindenmayer/evergreen/ module skeleton (query.py, surface.py, cli.py) plus a fixture event set covering all nine kinds, built with tests/relay_mock.py (already in-tree, reused by bridge and registry — do not write a new mock). fractal commit; this is the shared contract any child forks from.

3. Deliverable 1 (query library): nine kinds including 38110. Verification per sec 6.5 via core's module — signatures and attestation checked yourself, never the relay's word. No local index, no cache files: reconstruct on demand (relay-as-context). Acceptance includes a tampered-event rejection test.

4. Deliverable 2 (surface generator): derives from deliverable 1 ONLY — never Fractal's SQLite (condition 2; SQLite holds the step detail the wire format excludes, and the committed fixture would become a sec 6.1 leak). Model-policy display READS the live assignment; never restates tiers.

5. Deliverable 3 (CLI): stdlib argparse. The ARGUMENT PATH must be under test — an E2E invoking the module entry with real args for every subcommand. This is now dev-node template v3 rule; two prior nodes broke live on exactly this.

6. GATE: bash $NODE_DIR/scripts/test.sh green, including the committed mock-relay surface fixture. A live relay run is NOT your gate — but the interim dev relay IS currently reachable at ws://localhost:7100, so if your tests are green and budget remains, run the generator against it as a non-blocking bonus and report what you see. Do not block or retry on it.

7. Report observed per-iteration burn in your outbox — the architect is waiting on sonnet figures to recalibrate sec 7 cap sizing. Then finish in the same iteration your requirements hold.
---FEED---

aiTitle: Build Lindenmayer evergreen query library and CLI

content.text: Fresh node, no prior progress. Now let's read the required design docs and shipped APIs before building anything.

content.content:
203:## 5. The Evergreen Node
224:### 5.1 What evergreen is at v1
259:### 5.2 Session continuity (compaction)
297:## 6. Design principles

toolUseResult.stdout:
203:## 5. The Evergreen Node
224:### 5.1 What evergreen is at v1
259:### 5.2 Session continuity (compaction)
297:## 6. Design principles

content.content:
195	everything else: who granted what to whom, for which purpose, over which
196	window, and whether it was revoked are all facts in the event log, not rows
197	in a side policy store. Grants exist to facilitate fine-tuning a model for a
198	given task or project; the training itself runs on hosted inference/tuning
199	infra (Baseten/Fireworks-type) and is future development. Extraction
200	pipelines honor grants and their expiry/revocation, respecting §6.1
201	(mechanics open, §8).
202	
203	## 5. The Evergreen Node
204	
205	The **Evergreen Node** is the root node a human uses to manage their subgraph:
206	their durable, standing context environment. From it the human can:
207	
208	- commission trees on the fly,
209	- steer subgraphs (radio, signals, live contract edits),
210	- review approvals,
211	- query their own history.
212	
213	What the evergreen node sees is bounded by the platform's own wiring: radio
214	subscriptions are one hop, parent to direct child, so a subgraph's details
215	reach the evergreen root only as the aggregates each persistent child chooses
216	to publish upward. The aggregates-up privacy default (§6.1) is thereby
217	structurally enforced by the messaging topology itself, not policed after the
218	fact.
219	
220	The first Evergreen Node is handrolled at `tree/root/CONTEXT.md` and is the
221	living prototype: whatever that file needs to be is what Lindenmayer must
222	eventually generate and maintain automatically.
223	
224	### 5.1 What evergreen is at v1
225	
226	**Evergreen v1 is the read plane; Fractal's own CLI remains the write plane.**
227	v1 ships two things and no write path: a query surface over the signed log,
228	and a generator/maintainer of the standing context surface.
229	
230	The write-side gaps are real but they are not ours to fill. Radio send,
231	signals, live `NODE.md` edits, and node spawn are Fractal capabilities that
232	already exist and work; wrapping them would add a second control path with no
233	new capability, and an orchestration layer over Fractal's spawn API drifts
234	straight at §6.3. That signal routing is "Fractal CLI only" is not a gap —
235	it is the boundary holding. The read side is the opposite case: most of what
236	the prototype needs is reconstructable from the signed log, and none of it has
237	a query surface today.
238	
239	| §5 capability | v1 ships (read) | Stays Fractal CLI (write) |
240	|---|---|---|
241	| Commission trees | template catalog: versions, history, instance linkage | the spawn itself |
242	| Steer subgraphs | situational awareness: aggregates, budgets, pending gates | radio send, signals, live edits |
243	| Review approvals | the queue: 42040 pending, 42041 verdicts | the verdict (a radio reply) |
244	| Query own history | owned fully — this *is* the capability | — |
245	
246	The generated context surface is a composite: a human-authored preamble
247	(mission, phase, non-negotiables, governance mode, pointers) plus a generated
248	situational block derived from queries. That is what the handrolled prototype
249	already is, minus the hand maintenance.
250	
251	v1 holds no local index — it reconstructs from the relay and Fractal's SQLite
252	on demand, extending the relay-as-cursor (bridge) and relay-as-registry
253	(registry) precedents to **relay-as-context** (§6.2). Out of scope for v1, so
254	it is not silently dropped: node-spawn API, radio-send UI, live-edit UI,
255	signal API, consent-grant queries (§4 mechanics remain open, §8), and any
256	wire-visible decision-log kind — the decision log stays a section of this
257	manifest.
258	
259	### 5.2 Session continuity (compaction)
260	
261	Evergreen sessions compact, and a compaction is a derived view that must keep
262	a pointer to its source. Each compaction publishes a **kind 42060** event
263	carrying task coordinates in tags (`branch`, `run`, `iter`, `step`, the
264	session id, and the compacted span) plus an `e` tag with a `summary-of` marker
265	resolving to the step's own lifecycle event. Content carries metrics and a
266	**summary hash — never summary text**, and the pointer resolves to the node's
267	own step event, so a child's compaction stays in its subgraph and only
268	digests roll up (§6.1).
269	
270	**Detection reads the harness's own marker, which already exists.** Transcripts
271	carry an append-only `system` record with a `compactMetadata` object —
272	`trigger`, `preTokens`, `postTokens`, `durationMs`, `cumulativeDroppedTokens`,
273	and preserved-segment identifiers. Nothing upstream needs to change and
274	nothing needs inferring: `preTokens`/`postTokens` *are* the compaction metrics
275	42060 reports.
276	
277	A secondary signal corroborates it where the marker is absent (older
278	transcripts, or a harness that stops emitting it): total prompt size
279	(`input_tokens` + `cache_read_input_tokens` + `cache_creation_input_tokens`)
280	grows across a session and drops sharply at a compaction. The third term is
281	required — the two-term form tracks cache turnover, not context, and fires
282	several false positives per session as the prompt cache expires and refills.
283	
284	Every 42060 event therefore carries a `detection` tag recording which signal
285	produced it — `harness-marker` (primary, attested) or `usage-discontinuity`
286	(fallback, inferred) — so a reader can grade the claim rather than trust it
287	(§6.5).
288	
289	**Harvester boundary.** The bridge's transcript adapter may read append-only
290	structured *metadata* records, `compactMetadata` among them, in addition to
291	per-request usage fields. It still may not parse conversational structure, and
292	in particular must never read the compact summary body: the harness writes the
293	summary text into the transcript, and 42060 carries only its hash. This
294	extends the adapter's original usage-fields-only boundary; the prohibition
295	that matters — no conversational content — is unchanged.
296	
297	## 6. Design principles
298	
299	1. **Privacy-preserving defaults.** Human employees' full potential on this
300	   platform is only unlocked with a privacy-first approach; the goal is a
301	   collaboration platform for humans and AI as one integrated organizational
302	   graph. No arbitrary search of histories: access follows the graph —
303	   aggregates flow up, details stay in the subgraph. Privacy is a default,
304	   not a feature. Whether a subgraph publishes its raw node-session
305	   transcripts is a per-subgraph platform parameter (same family as the
306	   governance-mode parameter, §6.4); the platform-wide default is
307	   private-in-subgraph.
308	2. **No new storage systems.** The signed event log is the history. Retention
309	   is relay policy, per channel. Source-of-truth data stays in Fractal's
310	   SQLite and the signed event log on the Lindenmayer relay (§1; the Buzz
311	   relay carries the human workspace, never the record); parquet files in
312	   `data/` are snapshot exports for local review (duckdb), never sources of
313	   truth.
314	3. **Never patch or fork Fractal.** Integrate only through its extension
315	   surfaces; add no capabilities to Fractal nodes.
316	4. **Human governance is explicit and traceable.** Design review is a veto by
317	   default (a platform parameter); disagreements resolve as recorded
318	   conversation, never silent override.
319	5. **Relay enforcement is an optimization, never an assumption.** Client-side
320	   verification from the signed log is the security boundary. Buzz's
321	   relay-side enforcement — instant revocation cutoff, approval-gated
322	   merges, membership-checked writes, private-group read gating — is
323	   defense-in-depth that makes enforcement cheap when Buzz is deployed
324	   alongside, but it is Buzz-layer under §1's layering rule, and no client,
325	   bridge, or consumer may reason from it. Every consumer verifies from the
326	   signed log alone: attestation chains are checked before an author is
327	   trusted, approvals are counted before a merge is treated as approved,
328	   revoked keys are filtered at read time, and private-group read gating is
329	   treated as unverified until the deployment proves it. (Detail and the
330	   minimum relay contract: the relay-integration research aggregate,
331	   `docs/research/relay-integration/README.md`.)
332	
333	### Shadow cost
334	
335	All dollar figures in this project are *shadow costs*: real, server-reported
336	token counts priced at public API rates (Fractal's LiteLLM pricing table),
337	while execution actually runs on a Claude Max subscription. Nothing was
338	billed at these prices.
339	
340	- Token counts are ground truth (reported by the API in stream events, never
341	  estimated locally). Dollar figures are derived, notional, API-equivalent
342	  prices.
343	- Fractal's budget primitives (`max_cost`, reserve mode, cascading finish)
344	  operate on shadow cost and remain fully functional as steering and safety
345	  mechanisms — a budget cap is a real cap on work, even if the dollars are
346	  notional.
347	- Shadow cost is the right unit for the control plane's analytics: it is
348	  comparable across nodes, templates, models, and time, independent of how
349	  any given org pays (subscription, API, or enterprise agreement).
350	- The binding constraint on subscription execution is not dollars but rate
351	  windows; throttling events are recorded and reported separately from cost.
352	- Every exported dataset (parquet snapshots, demo dashboards) labels cost
353	  columns as shadow cost. No figure is presented as actual spend.
354	
355	## 7. Development & demo strategy
356	
357	- Developed by a Fractal tree from day one; the demo observes the tree that
358	  builds Lindenmayer, so dashboards show real subgraphs, merge-acceptance,
359	  and cost data.
360	- This dev tree is explicitly opted in to transcript publication (the
361	  per-subgraph parameter in §6.1): hook-synced node-session transcripts
362	  (`transcripts/`) are committed and published — for this tree they are the
363	  demo data. The platform-wide default remains private-in-subgraph.
364	- Decomposition principles for the dev tree: nodes own mergeable,
365	  directory-scoped artifacts; shared contracts land before parallel children
366	  spawn; the tree mirrors intended module boundaries; plan one ply and let
367	  nodes decide their own children; hand-authored Completion Requirements per
368	  NODE.md prefigure the evals pillar.
369	- **Decomposition economics rest on a ratio, not on specific tiers.** The
370	  doctrine holds because execution is cheaper than review and orchestration,
371	  and any tier assignment preserving that ordering preserves the doctrine
372	  (§3). What a retiering does change is absolute burn per child, so
373	  cap-sizing guidance — how large a leaf's cap must be to cover its solve plus
374	  wind-down — has to be restated against burn observed at the tiers actually
